author: bridge-bot
content: <yorickdowne> Did you even try to Google that? Bang this into a Google search prompt: “which version of tls supports quantum resistant algorithms?”

author: sky.cactus
content: My trust of the people in this room is much higher than my trust of Google results these days.  That being said, you are right that this is probably something more appropriate for an LLM chat, so I'll follow-up there.

author: _shemnon
content: There are some builds of TLS 1.3 that support hybrid x-wing style protocols, which are an EC algo (X25519, P256, P384) with ML-KEM (768 or 1024) - but like all tls you need to limit your accepted suites to prevent protocol downgrade by your counterpart.  TLS 1.2 is all EC IIRC

author: _shemnon
content: And PQC only matters durrng the handshake, AES (at 256 bits) is already quantum secure

author: bridge-bot
content:

> <_shemnon> There are some builds of TLS 1.3 that support hybrid x-wing style protocols, which are an EC algo (X25519, P256, P384) with ML-KEM (768 or 1024) - but like all tls you need to limit your accepted suites to prevent protocol downgrade by your counterpart.  TLS 1.2 is all EC IIRC
Also authentication still relies on the certificates which are RSA/ECDSA so no full quantum requests

author: _shemnon
content: Certs can be pure-pq or hybrid in 1.3, that is something you need to ensure if you are making a fully resistant path.  Browser support is still a work in progress.

author: bridge-bot
content:
<potuz0> re: **@postquantum_bot**
> <_shemnon> Certs can be pure-pq or hybrid in 1.3, that is something you need to ensure if you are making a fully resistant path.  Browser support is still a work in progress.
yes in theory 1.3 accepts pq certs, but the whole PKI ecosystem needs to change.

author: _shemnon
content: I think that's the signal that needs to be surfaced.  Solutions exist, they are not being used.  Add WiFi gear to that list too, as WPA3 is entirely EC dependent.
